main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Prototype Consolidation and Ensemble-based Exemplar-Free Deep Incremental Learning (ProCEED)","Dynamic realignment of previous class representations in the latest subspace to adjust drifted class prototypes","Computation of inter-subspace angular drifts to adjust old task prototypes","Knowledge distillation from a mixture of experts (MoE) where a single expert is fine-tuned and inherits knowledge from others without explicit optimization","Prototype-based ensemble of deep Gaussian classifiers during inference"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in exemplar-free class incremental learning (EFCIL)","Prototype drift due to subspace changes in incremental learning","Balancing stability-plasticity dilemma in deep incremental learning","Maintaining past knowledge without access to previous task samples"],"datasets":["CIFAR100/20","Other challenging benchmark datasets (not explicitly named in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy","value":"ProCEED demonstrates dominant accuracy compared to state-of-the-art class-incremental learning methods (exact values not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of ProCEED, a novel method for prototype consolidation and ensemble-based exemplar-free deep incremental learning","Realignment of old task representations into the latest subspace to prevent feature drift and forgetting, without extra optimization overhead","A new knowledge distillation technique where a single expert inherits knowledge from a mixture of experts without optimizing the entire ensemble","Use of a prototype-based ensemble of deep Gaussian classifiers for improved plasticity and generalization in incremental learning"]}

methods: Prototype Consolidation and Ensemble-based Exemplar-Free Deep Incremental Learning (ProCEED)
Dynamic realignment of previous class representations in the latest subspace to adjust drifted class prototypes
Computation of inter-subspace angular drifts to adjust old task prototypes
Knowledge distillation from a mixture of experts (MoE) where a single expert is fine-tuned and inherits knowledge from others without explicit optimization
Prototype-based ensemble of deep Gaussian classifiers during inference
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in exemplar-free class incremental learning (EFCIL)
Prototype drift due to subspace changes in incremental learning
Balancing stability-plasticity dilemma in deep incremental learning
Maintaining past knowledge without access to previous task samples
datasets: Other challenging benchmark datasets (not explicitly named in the provided text)

results.value: ProCEED demonstrates dominant accuracy compared to state-of-the-art class-incremental learning methods (exact values not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Introduction of ProCEED, a novel method for prototype consolidation and ensemble-based exemplar-free deep incremental learning
Realignment of old task representations into the latest subspace to prevent feature drift and forgetting, without extra optimization overhead
A new knowledge distillation technique where a single expert inherits knowledge from a mixture of experts without optimizing the entire ensemble
Use of a prototype-based ensemble of deep Gaussian classifiers for improved plasticity and generalization in incremental learning

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Expandable Subspace Ensemble (EASE): trains a distinct lightweight adapter module for each new task to create task-specific subspaces, enabling joint decision-making across multiple subspaces.","Semantic-guided prototype complement strategy: synthesizes old classes' new features without using any old class instance, using class-wise similarities in the co-occurrence space to guide classifier mapping."],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning (CIL) when using pre-trained models (PTMs).","Efficient model updating in CIL without harming former knowledge (stability-plasticity dilemma).","Constructing low-cost, task-specific subspaces for incremental learning without high resource allocation.","Developing classifiers that can map continuously expanding features to corresponding classes without using exemplars."],"datasets":["Seven benchmark datasets (not all named in the provided text, but implied to include standard CIL datasets such as CIFAR-100, ImageNet, etc.)"],"metrics":["Average Accuracy (%)","Trainable Parameters (%)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average Accuracy (%)","value":"EASE achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to prior methods (e.g., FOSTER, MEMO, DualPrompt, etc.) on seven benchmark datasets. (Exact numbers not provided in the text, but EASE outperforms others as shown in Figure 1.)"},{"metric":"Trainable Parameters (%)","value":"EASE achieves high accuracy with limited memory cost (exact percentage not specified in the text)."}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new expandable subspace ensemble (EASE) method for PTM-based CIL that uses lightweight adapters to create task-specific subspaces, avoiding cross-task conflict and catastrophic forgetting.","Introduces a semantic-guided prototype complement strategy to synthesize old class features and classifiers without using any exemplars.","Achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven benchmark datasets with negligible training and memory costs."]}

methods: Expandable Subspace Ensemble (EASE): trains a distinct lightweight adapter module for each new task to create task-specific subspaces, enabling joint decision-making across multiple subspaces.

Semantic-guided prototype complement strategy: synthesizes old classes' new features without using any old class instance, using class-wise similarities in the co-occurrence space to guide classifier mapping.
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning (CIL) when using pre-trained models (PTMs).
Efficient model updating in CIL without harming former knowledge (stability-plasticity dilemma).
Constructing low-cost, task-specific subspaces for incremental learning without high resource allocation.
Developing classifiers that can map continuously expanding features to corresponding classes without using exemplars.
datasets: Seven benchmark datasets (not all named in the provided text, but implied to include standard CIL datasets such as CIFAR-100, ImageNet, etc.)

results.value: EASE achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to prior methods (e.g., FOSTER, MEMO, DualPrompt, etc.) on seven benchmark datasets. (Exact numbers not provided in the text, but EASE outperforms others as shown in Figure 1.)
EASE achieves high accuracy with limited memory cost (exact percentage not specified in the text).

novelty_claims: Proposes a new expandable subspace ensemble (EASE) method for PTM-based CIL that uses lightweight adapters to create task-specific subspaces, avoiding cross-task conflict and catastrophic forgetting.
Introduces a semantic-guided prototype complement strategy to synthesize old class features and classifiers without using any exemplars.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven benchmark datasets with negligible training and memory costs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Representation-Enhanced Analytic Learning (REAL)","Dual-stream base pretraining (combining self-supervised contrastive learning and supervised learning)","Representation enhancing distillation","Feature fusion buffer for multi-layer backbone features"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning without exemplars (exemplar-free CIL)","Ineffective representations in analytic continual learning (ACL)","Insufficient utilization of backbone knowledge in ACL"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100","ImageNet-100","ImageNet-1k"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy (CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, ImageNet-1k)","value":"REAL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming exemplar-free methods and rivaling exemplar-based approaches"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a representation-enhanced analytic learning (REAL) framework for exemplar-free class-incremental learning.","Introduces dual-stream base pretraining combining self-supervised contrastive learning and supervised learning for better representations.","Develops a representation enhancing distillation process to merge general and class-specific features.","Presents a feature fusion buffer to utilize multi-layer backbone features for improved classifier training.","REAL can be incorporated into existing analytic continual learning techniques and significantly improves performance."]}

methods: Representation-Enhanced Analytic Learning (REAL)
Dual-stream base pretraining (combining self-supervised contrastive learning and supervised learning)
Representation enhancing distillation
Feature fusion buffer for multi-layer backbone features
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning without exemplars (exemplar-free CIL)
Ineffective representations in analytic continual learning (ACL)
Insufficient utilization of backbone knowledge in ACL

results.value: REAL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming exemplar-free methods and rivaling exemplar-based approaches

novelty_claims: Proposes a representation-enhanced analytic learning (REAL) framework for exemplar-free class-incremental learning.
Introduces dual-stream base pretraining combining self-supervised contrastive learning and supervised learning for better representations.
Develops a representation enhancing distillation process to merge general and class-specific features.
Presents a feature fusion buffer to utilize multi-layer backbone features for improved classifier training.
REAL can be incorporated into existing analytic continual learning techniques and significantly improves performance.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Self-sustaining representation expansion scheme","Dynamic structure reorganization (DSR) strategy","Main-branch distillation (MBD)","Prototype selection mechanism (PSM)","Residual adapter insertion in feature extractor","Structural reparameterization technique"],"problems":["Non-exemplar class-incremental learning (NECIL) where old class samples cannot be saved","Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning without exemplars","Feature confusion and representation overlap between old and new classes in incremental learning"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100","TinyImageNet","ImageNetSubset"],"metrics":["Incremental performance (accuracy)","Performance margin over state-of-the-art methods"],"results":[{"metric":"Incremental performance improvement over state-of-the-art","value":"3% (CIFAR-100), 3% (TinyImageNet), 6% (ImageNetSubset)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a self-sustaining representation expansion scheme for non-exemplar incremental learning, enabling cyclically expanding optimization and structure-invariant representation.","Introduces a dynamic structure reorganization strategy that fuses main-branch expansion and side-branch updating to maintain old features.","Develops a main-branch distillation scheme to transfer invariant knowledge from old to new classes.","Proposes a prototype selection mechanism that reduces feature confusion by selectively incorporating new samples into the distillation process.","Demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods on three benchmark datasets."]}

methods: Self-sustaining representation expansion scheme
Dynamic structure reorganization (DSR) strategy
Main-branch distillation (MBD)
Prototype selection mechanism (PSM)
Residual adapter insertion in feature extractor
Structural reparameterization technique
problems: Non-exemplar class-incremental learning (NECIL) where old class samples cannot be saved
Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning without exemplars
Feature confusion and representation overlap between old and new classes in incremental learning

results.value: 3% (CIFAR-100), 3% (TinyImageNet), 6% (ImageNetSubset)

novelty_claims: Proposes a self-sustaining representation expansion scheme for non-exemplar incremental learning, enabling cyclically expanding optimization and structure-invariant representation.
Introduces a dynamic structure reorganization strategy that fuses main-branch expansion and side-branch updating to maintain old features.
Develops a main-branch distillation scheme to transfer invariant knowledge from old to new classes.
Proposes a prototype selection mechanism that reduces feature confusion by selectively incorporating new samples into the distillation process.
Demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods on three benchmark datasets.

problems: Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning without access to previous data

novelty_claims: Proposing the removal of the batch normalization layer (RBNL) during incremental training to improve representation stability in class-incremental learning.

Introducing prototype mixing (PM) to expand the feature space by generating hybrid prototypes with composite labels, enhancing the model's ability to balance stability and plasticity without using exemplars.
Achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets in the exemplar-free class-incremental learning setting.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SEED (Selection of Experts for Ensemble Diversification): an ensemble method for continual learning where only one expert is selectively trained per new task, using multivariate Gaussian distributions for class representation and expert selection, and Bayes classification for inference"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning (CIL)","High computational burden and lack of diversity in mixture-of-expert approaches for CIL","Limitations of exemplar-free CIL methods, especially when little data is available upfront","Need for efficient, scalable, and diverse expert ensembles in continual learning without storing exemplars"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Accuracy (state-of-the-art accuracy for task-aware and task-agnostic scenarios)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of SEED, a new ensemble method for exemplar-free continual learning that selectively trains only one expert per new task, mitigating forgetting and encouraging expert diversification without computational overhead.","A unique expert selection mechanism based on multivariate Gaussian distributions of class representations, which limits representational drift and enables task-agnostic Bayes classification at inference.","SEED achieves state-of-the-art performance in exemplar-free settings across various scenarios, outperforming other methods without relying on strong feature extractors or assumptions about class distribution during incremental learning."]}

methods: SEED (Selection of Experts for Ensemble Diversification): an ensemble method for continual learning where only one expert is selectively trained per new task, using multivariate Gaussian distributions for class representation and expert selection, and Bayes classification for inference
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning (CIL)
High computational burden and lack of diversity in mixture-of-expert approaches for CIL
Limitations of exemplar-free CIL methods, especially when little data is available upfront
Need for efficient, scalable, and diverse expert ensembles in continual learning without storing exemplars
metrics: Accuracy (state-of-the-art accuracy for task-aware and task-agnostic scenarios)
novelty_claims: Introduction of SEED, a new ensemble method for exemplar-free continual learning that selectively trains only one expert per new task, mitigating forgetting and encouraging expert diversification without computational overhead.

A unique expert selection mechanism based on multivariate Gaussian distributions of class representations, which limits representational drift and enables task-agnostic Bayes classification at inference.

SEED achieves state-of-the-art performance in exemplar-free settings across various scenarios, outperforming other methods without relying on strong feature extractors or assumptions about class distribution during incremental learning.

novelty_claims: First to highlight the need for adjusting the teacher model in exemplar-free CIL, where it is usually kept frozen.
Propose Teacher Adaptation (TA), a simple and effective technique that updates the teacher's batch normalization statistics during incremental training.
First application of adapting the teacher model (via batch normalization statistics) to the CIL scenario with non-overlapping data between teacher and student.

Demonstrate that TA can be seamlessly integrated with various KD-based CIL methods, consistently improving performance across multiple datasets and settings, including with pretrained models and under substantial distributional shifts.

Introduces the Empirical Feature Matrix (EFM) to induce a pseudo-metric in feature space for regularizing feature drift, which is more tractable than the Fisher Information Matrix.

Comprehensive analysis of the impact of oversampling and feature pool diversification on EFCIL performance.
Introduction of dynamic recalibration to better align pseudo-features with shifting data distributions during incremental learning.
Nuanced exploration of the relationship between feature density, oversampling, and incremental learning efficacy.
Demonstration of superior performance over ten recent EFCIL methods without using exemplars.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FeTrIL: Feature Translation for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning, which combines a frozen feature extractor and a pseudo-feature generator based on geometric translation of new class features to create pseudo-features for past classes; only centroids of past classes are stored; a linear classifier is trained incrementally on actual features of new classes and pseudo-features of past classes"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL)","Balancing stability and plasticity in incremental learning without access to past data (exemplar-free)","Reducing memory and computational costs in class-incremental learning"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100","ImageNet-Subset","TinyImageNet"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy (various settings)","value":"FeTrIL outperforms ten existing methods in most cases across three challenging datasets"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new EFCIL method (FeTrIL) that combines a frozen feature extractor with a pseudo-feature generator based on geometric translation of new class features to represent past classes","The pseudo-feature generation only requires storage of class centroids, making it memory efficient","The method achieves a better balance between stability and plasticity compared to previous EFCIL approaches","FeTrIL is much faster than mainstream methods that update the entire deep model during incremental learning"]}

methods: FeTrIL: Feature Translation for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning, which combines a frozen feature extractor and a pseudo-feature generator based on geometric translation of new class features to create pseudo-features for past classes; only centroids of past classes are stored; a linear classifier is trained incrementally on actual features of new classes and pseudo-features of past classes
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL)
Balancing stability and plasticity in incremental learning without access to past data (exemplar-free)
Reducing memory and computational costs in class-incremental learning

results.value: FeTrIL outperforms ten existing methods in most cases across three challenging datasets

novelty_claims: Proposes a new EFCIL method (FeTrIL) that combines a frozen feature extractor with a pseudo-feature generator based on geometric translation of new class features to represent past classes
The pseudo-feature generation only requires storage of class centroids, making it memory efficient
The method achieves a better balance between stability and plasticity compared to previous EFCIL approaches
FeTrIL is much faster than mainstream methods that update the entire deep model during incremental learning

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SimpleCIL (simple baseline using frozen PTM and prototype-based classifier)","AdaPt (parameter-efficient tuning of PTM in the first incremental stage)","mERge (Aper): Aggregates embeddings from PTM and adapted models for classifier construction; a general framework that can be combined with any parameter-efficient tuning method"],"problems":["Class-incremental learning (CIL) with pre-trained models (PTMs)","Catastrophic forgetting when learning new classes sequentially","Domain gap between pre-trained and downstream datasets in CIL","Inadequacy of traditional ImageNet-based CIL benchmarks due to data overlap with PTM pre-training"],"datasets":["ImageNet-A","ObjectNet","OmniBenchmark","VTAB"],"metrics":["Average accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Average accuracy (VTAB)","value":"SimpleCIL outperforms current SOTA by 5% even without any tuning"},{"metric":"Average accuracy (VTAB)","value":"Aper consistently improves over SimpleCIL with minimal additional cost"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that a simple baseline (SimpleCIL) using frozen PTM and prototype-based classifier can outperform state-of-the-art CIL methods without any downstream training, highlighting the generalizability of PTMs.","Proposes Aper, a unified framework that combines the generalizability of PTMs and the adaptivity of adapted models via parameter-efficient tuning and embedding aggregation, applicable to various network structures and tuning techniques.","Introduces four new CIL benchmarks (ImageNet-A, ObjectNet, OmniBenchmark, VTAB) with large domain gaps from ImageNet to better evaluate PTM-based CIL methods, addressing the overlap issue in traditional benchmarks."]}

methods: SimpleCIL (simple baseline using frozen PTM and prototype-based classifier)
AdaPt (parameter-efficient tuning of PTM in the first incremental stage)
mERge (Aper): Aggregates embeddings from PTM and adapted models for classifier construction; a general framework that can be combined with any parameter-efficient tuning method
problems: Class-incremental learning (CIL) with pre-trained models (PTMs)
Catastrophic forgetting when learning new classes sequentially
Domain gap between pre-trained and downstream datasets in CIL
Inadequacy of traditional ImageNet-based CIL benchmarks due to data overlap with PTM pre-training

results.value: SimpleCIL outperforms current SOTA by 5% even without any tuning
Aper consistently improves over SimpleCIL with minimal additional cost

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that a simple baseline (SimpleCIL) using frozen PTM and prototype-based classifier can outperform state-of-the-art CIL methods without any downstream training, highlighting the generalizability of PTMs.

Proposes Aper, a unified framework that combines the generalizability of PTMs and the adaptivity of adapted models via parameter-efficient tuning and embedding aggregation, applicable to various network structures and tuning techniques.

Introduces four new CIL benchmarks (ImageNet-A, ObjectNet, OmniBenchmark, VTAB) with large domain gaps from ImageNet to better evaluate PTM-based CIL methods, addressing the overlap issue in traditional benchmarks.

methods: RanPAC: Random Projections and Pre-trained Models for Continual Learning
Injection of a frozen Random Projection layer with nonlinear activation between pre-trained model features and output head
Class-prototype accumulation (CP) strategy
Decorrelation of class-prototypes using second-order feature statistics
Combination with Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning (PETL) methods
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, especially with pre-trained models
Bridging distribution gaps when using pre-extracted features from pre-trained models
Maintaining performance on old tasks while learning new tasks in a non-stationary data stream
Improving class-incremental and domain-incremental continual learning without rehearsal memory
datasets: Seven class-incremental benchmarks (exact list not fully specified in the excerpt, but CIFAR-100 is explicitly mentioned)

results.value: 20% to 62% lower than previous methods on seven class-incremental benchmarks using pre-trained ViT-B/16, without rehearsal memory
Highest-to-date on all tested class-incremental and domain-incremental datasets with pre-trained ViT-B/16

novelty_claims: Proposes a concise and effective approach for continual learning with pre-trained models using training-free random projectors and class-prototype accumulation, bypassing catastrophic forgetting.
Introduces a frozen Random Projection layer with nonlinear activation to enhance linear separability for class-prototype-based continual learning.
Demonstrates the importance of decorrelating class-prototypes to reduce distribution disparity when using pre-trained representations.
Achieves state-of-the-art rehearsal-free continual learning accuracies on all tested class-incremental and domain-incremental datasets with pre-trained ViT-B/16.
Shows that the full potential of pre-trained models for simple, effective, and fast continual learning has not been fully tapped before.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Adversarial Drift Compensation (ADC): adversarially perturbing new task samples so their embeddings are close to old class prototypes, then using these adversarial samples to estimate and compensate for prototype drift in the embedding space","Use of class prototypes with a Nearest Class Mean (NCM) classifier","Logits distillation baseline with NCM classifier"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, especially in exemplar-free continual learning (EFCIL) settings","Feature drift estimation and compensation in the absence of stored exemplars","Poor performance of EFCIL methods when the initial task is small (small-start setting)"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100","TinyImageNet","ImageNet-Subset","CUB-200","Stanford Cars"],"metrics":["Last task accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Last task accuracy (fine-grained datasets)","value":"~9% improvement over state-of-the-art methods"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Adversarial Drift Compensation (ADC), a novel method to estimate and compensate for semantic drift in feature space using adversarially generated pseudo-exemplars, without storing real exemplars","Demonstrates that adversarial samples can serve as effective pseudo-exemplars for drift estimation in continual learning","Shows that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing EFCIL methods, especially in the challenging small-start setting and on fine-grained datasets"]}

methods: Adversarial Drift Compensation (ADC): adversarially perturbing new task samples so their embeddings are close to old class prototypes, then using these adversarial samples to estimate and compensate for prototype drift in the embedding space
Use of class prototypes with a Nearest Class Mean (NCM) classifier
Logits distillation baseline with NCM classifier
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, especially in exemplar-free continual learning (EFCIL) settings
Feature drift estimation and compensation in the absence of stored exemplars
Poor performance of EFCIL methods when the initial task is small (small-start setting)

results.value: ~9% improvement over state-of-the-art methods

novelty_claims: Proposes Adversarial Drift Compensation (ADC), a novel method to estimate and compensate for semantic drift in feature space using adversarially generated pseudo-exemplars, without storing real exemplars
Demonstrates that adversarial samples can serve as effective pseudo-exemplars for drift estimation in continual learning
Shows that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing EFCIL methods, especially in the challenging small-start setting and on fine-grained datasets

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Co2L (Contrastive Continual Learning): a rehearsal-based continual learning algorithm that (1) learns representations using an asymmetric supervised contrastive loss, and (2) preserves learned representations using a self-supervised distillation step (instance-wise relation distillation)"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning","Learning and preserving transferable representations in continual learning","Maintaining representation quality without a jointly trained classifier in continual learning"],"datasets":["Seq-CIFAR-10","Other popular benchmark image classification datasets (not explicitly named in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Relative improvement (%)"],"results":[{"metric":"Relative improvement (distillation, with buffered samples) on Seq-CIFAR-10","value":"22.40%"},{"metric":"Relative improvement (distillation, without buffered samples) on Seq-CIFAR-10","value":"10.59%"},{"metric":"Relative improvement (asymmetric supervised contrastive loss, buffer size 500, Seq-CIFAR-10)","value":"8.15%"}],"novelty_claims":["First method to explicitly preserve contrastively learned representations in continual learning without a jointly trained classifier.","Proposes an asymmetric supervised contrastive loss tailored for continual learning.","Introduces a novel self-distillation mechanism (instance-wise relation distillation) to preserve learned representations.","Demonstrates that contrastively learned representations are more robust to catastrophic forgetting than cross-entropy based methods in continual learning."]}

methods: Co2L (Contrastive Continual Learning): a rehearsal-based continual learning algorithm that (1) learns representations using an asymmetric supervised contrastive loss, and (2) preserves learned representations using a self-supervised distillation step (instance-wise relation distillation)
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning
Learning and preserving transferable representations in continual learning
Maintaining representation quality without a jointly trained classifier in continual learning
datasets: Other popular benchmark image classification datasets (not explicitly named in the provided text)

results.metric: Relative improvement (distillation, with buffered samples) on Seq-CIFAR-10
Relative improvement (distillation, without buffered samples) on Seq-CIFAR-10
Relative improvement (asymmetric supervised contrastive loss, buffer size 500, Seq-CIFAR-10)

novelty_claims: First method to explicitly preserve contrastively learned representations in continual learning without a jointly trained classifier.
Proposes an asymmetric supervised contrastive loss tailored for continual learning.
Introduces a novel self-distillation mechanism (instance-wise relation distillation) to preserve learned representations.
Demonstrates that contrastively learned representations are more robust to catastrophic forgetting than cross-entropy based methods in continual learning.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["BiC (Bias Correction) method: a two-stage approach where a bias correction layer (a simple linear model with two parameters) is added after the last fully connected layer of a CNN to correct bias towards new classes in incremental learning. The method splits data into training and validation sets, using the validation set to estimate and correct the bias."],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning when new classes are added and old class data is missing.","Performance degradation in large scale incremental learning due to data imbalance between old and new classes.","Difficulty in distinguishing an increasing number of visually similar classes under data imbalance."],"datasets":["ImageNet (ILSVRC 2012, 1000 classes)","MS-Celeb-1M (10000 classes)","ImageNet-100 (100 classes)","ImageNet-1000 (1000 classes)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Performance degradation (gap between incremental and non-incremental classifier accuracy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art on ImageNet","value":"11.1% higher"},{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art on MS-Celeb-1M","value":"13.2% higher"}],"novelty_claims":["Identification of strong bias towards new classes in the last fully connected layer of CNNs during incremental learning.","Proposal of a simple bias correction layer (BiC) with only two parameters to correct this bias.","Demonstration that BiC significantly reduces performance degradation in large scale incremental learning, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods by a large margin on large datasets."]}

methods: BiC (Bias Correction) method: a two-stage approach where a bias correction layer (a simple linear model with two parameters) is added after the last fully connected layer of a CNN to correct bias towards new classes in incremental learning. The method splits data into training and validation sets, using the validation set to estimate and correct the bias.
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in incremental learning when new classes are added and old class data is missing.
Performance degradation in large scale incremental learning due to data imbalance between old and new classes.
Difficulty in distinguishing an increasing number of visually similar classes under data imbalance.
metrics: Performance degradation (gap between incremental and non-incremental classifier accuracy)

results.metric: Accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art on ImageNet
results.value: 11.1% higher

results.metric: Accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art on MS-Celeb-1M
results.value: 13.2% higher

novelty_claims: Identification of strong bias towards new classes in the last fully connected layer of CNNs during incremental learning.
Proposal of a simple bias correction layer (BiC) with only two parameters to correct this bias.
Demonstration that BiC significantly reduces performance degradation in large scale incremental learning, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods by a large margin on large datasets.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Compacting, Picking, and Growing (CPG) continual learning method","Gradual pruning for model compression","Critical weights selection via learnable binary mask","Progressive network expansion (growing) when necessary"],"problems":["Catastrophic forgetting in continual/lifelong learning","Scalability of deep models to an unlimited number of sequential tasks","Maintaining model compactness while learning new tasks","Leveraging accumulated knowledge for new tasks without access to previous data"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Integration of model compression (compacting), critical weights selection (picking), and progressive network expansion (growing) in a single continual learning framework (CPG)","Ability to avoid forgetting by fixing old-task weights and only adapting new/released weights","Selective reuse of critical old-task weights via a differentiable mask, rather than reusing all old weights","Maintaining model compactness while allowing for expansion only when necessary, enabling scalability to unlimited sequential tasks","Demonstration that accumulated knowledge from previous tasks can enhance learning of new tasks compared to independent training"]}

methods: Compacting, Picking, and Growing (CPG) continual learning method
Gradual pruning for model compression
Critical weights selection via learnable binary mask
Progressive network expansion (growing) when necessary
problems: Catastrophic forgetting in continual/lifelong learning
Scalability of deep models to an unlimited number of sequential tasks
Maintaining model compactness while learning new tasks
Leveraging accumulated knowledge for new tasks without access to previous data
novelty_claims: Integration of model compression (compacting), critical weights selection (picking), and progressive network expansion (growing) in a single continual learning framework (CPG)
Ability to avoid forgetting by fixing old-task weights and only adapting new/released weights
Selective reuse of critical old-task weights via a differentiable mask, rather than reusing all old weights
Maintaining model compactness while allowing for expansion only when necessary, enabling scalability to unlimited sequential tasks
Demonstration that accumulated knowledge from previous tasks can enhance learning of new tasks compared to independent training
